Arguments.prompt: Download the US state boundary shapefile from the US Census Bureau at https://www2.census.gov/geo/tiger/GENZ2018/shp/cb_2018_us_state_20m.zip. Unzip the archive and load the shapefile using geopandas. Filter the GeoDataFrame to the row where the NAME field equals 'Oregon'. Reproject the Oregon geometry to EPSG:4326 (geographic coordinates, WGS84) if it is not already in that CRS. Compute the bounding box of the Oregon state geometry using the .total_bounds attribute, which returns (minx, miny, maxx, maxy) corresponding to (min_lon, min_lat, max_lon, max_lat). Save the Oregon state boundary as OregonState.geojson in GeoJSON format. Also download the US county boundary shapefile from https://www2.census.gov/geo/tiger/GENZ2018/shp/cb_2018_us_county_5m.zip, unzip it, load it with geopandas, filter to rows where the STATEFP field matches Oregon's STATEFP value, reproject to EPSG:4326, and save as OregonCounties.geojson. Save a plain text file named OregonBBox.txt containing exactly four lines: line 1 is min_lon, line 2 is min_lat, line 3 is max_lon, line 4 is max_lat, each as a floating-point number. Upload OregonState.geojson, OregonCounties.geojson, and OregonBBox.txt to the data store under the folder OregonNDVI.

Download OregonBBox.txt from the data store folder OregonNDVI. Read the four lines to get min_lon, min_lat, max_lon, max_lat as floats representing Oregon's bounding box. Authenticate with NASA Earthdata using credentials stored in environment variables EARTHDATA_USERNAME and EARTHDATA_PASSWORD. Use the NASA CMR API at https://cmr.earthdata.nasa.gov/search/granules.json to search for MODIS MOD13A2 version 061 granules. For each of the past 12 calendar months (from 12 months ago through last month, relative to today's date), issue a CMR search request with parameters: short_name=MOD13A2, version=061, temporal=<YYYY-MM-01T00:00:00Z,YYYY-MM-LastDayT23:59:59Z> for that month, bounding_box=<min_lon,min_lat,max_lon,max_lat>. From the results, select exactly one granule per month (the first result returned). Collect the download URL for each granule from the links array where the rel field contains 'data' and the href ends in '.hdf'. Download each HDF file using an authenticated session (requests.Session with HTTPBasicAuth using EARTHDATA_USERNAME and EARTHDATA_PASSWORD, following redirects). Save each file as MOD13A2_<YYYY>_<MM>.hdf where YYYY and MM are the year and two-digit month. Upload all 12 HDF files to the data store under the folder OregonNDVI/raw. Also save a text file named granule_list.txt listing one filename per line for all 12 downloaded files, and upload it to OregonNDVI.

Download OregonState.geojson and OregonBBox.txt from the data store folder OregonNDVI. Download granule_list.txt from OregonNDVI and read the list of 12 HDF filenames. Download each HDF file listed in granule_list.txt from the data store folder OregonNDVI/raw. For each HDF file MOD13A2_<YYYY>_<MM>.hdf, perform the following steps using the pyhdf or rasterio library: (1) Open the HDF file and access the subdataset named '1 km 16 days NDVI' to extract the raw NDVI raster array. (2) Access the subdataset named '1 km 16 days VI Quality' to extract the QA raster array. (3) Read the spatial reference and geotransform from the HDF metadata. The MODIS sinusoidal projection is EPSG:6842 or use the proj4 string '+proj=sinu +R=6371007.181 +nadgrids=@null +wktext'. (4) Scale the raw NDVI integer values to the range [-1, 1] by multiplying by 0.0001 (the official MODIS NDVI scale factor). (5) Parse the QA band: mask out pixels where the VI usefulness bits (bits 2-5) indicate water (bit pattern where land/water flag bits 11-13 equal 001 for water), snow/ice (bits 14-15 equal 01), or where the overall VI quality bits (bits 0-1) equal 11 (not produced due to other reason) or 10 (pixel produced but most probably cloudy). Specifically, mask pixels where (QA & 0x0003) >= 2 (low quality or not produced), or where (QA & 0x0C00) >> 10 == 1 (mixed clouds), or where (QA & 0x6000) >> 13 == 1 (adjacent to snow). Set masked pixel NDVI values to NaN. (6) Reproject the masked NDVI raster from the MODIS sinusoidal projection to EPSG:4326 using rasterio.warp.reproject with Resampling.bilinear. (7) Clip the reprojected raster to the Oregon state boundary polygon from OregonState.geojson using rasterio.mask.mask. (8) Compute the mean NDVI across all non-NaN pixels in the clipped raster. Record the mean value along with the year and month. After processing all 12 files, save a CSV file named monthly_mean_ndvi.csv with columns: year (integer), month (integer, 1-12), mean_ndvi (float, rounded to 6 decimal places), date (string in format YYYY-MM-01). Sort rows by year and month ascending. Also save each clipped, masked, scaled NDVI raster as a GeoTIFF file named ndvi_clipped_<YYYY>_<MM>.tif in EPSG:4326 with float32 dtype and nodata=-9999 (replacing NaN with -9999). Upload monthly_mean_ndvi.csv to OregonNDVI and upload all 12 GeoTIFF files to OregonNDVI/processed.

Download OregonState.geojson and OregonCounties.geojson from the data store folder OregonNDVI. Download monthly_mean_ndvi.csv from OregonNDVI. Read the CSV which has columns: year, month, mean_ndvi, date. Download all 12 GeoTIFF files named ndvi_clipped_<YYYY>_<MM>.tif from the data store folder OregonNDVI/processed, where YYYY and MM correspond to the 12 rows in monthly_mean_ndvi.csv sorted by year and month ascending. Create a matplotlib figure with 12 subplots arranged in a 3-row by 4-column grid, figsize=(20, 15). For each subplot (indexed 0 through 11 in chronological order by year and month): (1) Open the corresponding ndvi_clipped_<YYYY>_<MM>.tif using rasterio. Read band 1 as a float32 array. Replace nodata values (-9999) with NaN. (2) Get the raster extent (left, bottom, right, top) from the rasterio transform and shape for use as the extent parameter in imshow. (3) Plot the NDVI array using ax.imshow with cmap='YlGn', vmin=-0.2, vmax=1.0, extent=[left, right, bottom, top], origin='upper', aspect='auto'. (4) Overlay the Oregon state boundary from OregonState.geojson using geopandas plot on the same axes with facecolor='none', edgecolor='black', linewidth=1.5. (5) Overlay Oregon county boundaries from OregonCounties.geojson with facecolor='none', edgecolor='gray', linewidth=0.5. (6) Set the subplot title to the month name and year, e.g. 'January 2024', using calendar.month_name[month] and the year value from the CSV. (7) Set x-axis label to 'Longitude' and y-axis label to 'Latitude'. (8) Set axis limits to Oregon's bounding box: xlim from min_lon to max_lon, ylim from min_lat to max_lat, read from OregonBBox.txt in OregonNDVI. After all 12 subplots are drawn, add a single shared colorbar on the right side of the figure labeled 'NDVI' using matplotlib.colorbar. Set the overall figure supertitle to 'Monthly NDVI Distribution Across Oregon (Past 12 Months)' with fontsize=16. Use plt.tight_layout() before saving. Save the figure as oregon_ndvi_map_grid.png at dpi=150. Upload oregon_ndvi_map_grid.png to the data store under folder OregonNDVI/output.

Download monthly_mean_ndvi.csv from the data store folder OregonNDVI. Read the CSV which has columns: year (integer), month (integer), mean_ndvi (float), date (string YYYY-MM-01). Parse the date column as datetime objects using pandas.to_datetime. Sort by date ascending. Create a matplotlib figure with a single axes, figsize=(12, 6). Plot the mean_ndvi values as a line with markers: use plt.plot(dates, mean_ndvi_values, color='green', linewidth=2, marker='o', markersize=6, label='Mean NDVI'). Identify seasonal peaks: find all local maxima in the mean_ndvi series where a value is greater than both its immediate neighbors (or is the maximum of the series if only one peak exists). For each local maximum, draw a vertical dashed line using ax.axvline at that date with color='orange', linestyle='--', alpha=0.7, linewidth=1.5, and annotate the point with the text 'Peak: {value:.3f}' using ax.annotate with xytext offset (0, 10) in points, fontsize=9, color='darkorange', ha='center'. Identify seasonal troughs: find all local minima where a value is less than both its immediate neighbors. For each local minimum, draw a vertical dashed line using ax.axvline at that date with color='steelblue', linestyle='--', alpha=0.7, linewidth=1.5, and annotate with 'Trough: {value:.3f}' using ax.annotate with xytext offset (0, -15) in points, fontsize=9, color='steelblue', ha='center'. Set x-axis tick labels to the month abbreviation and year for each of the 12 data points using ax.set_xticks(dates) and ax.set_xticklabels([d.strftime('%b %Y') for d in dates], rotation=45, ha='right'). Set y-axis limits to [-0.1, 1.0]. Set x-axis label to 'Month', y-axis label to 'Mean NDVI', and title to 'Mean Statewide NDVI Over Time - Oregon (Past 12 Months)'. Add a legend with loc='upper right'. Add a horizontal grid with ax.yaxis.grid(True, linestyle='--', alpha=0.5). Use plt.tight_layout(). Save the figure as oregon_ndvi_timeseries.png at dpi=150. Upload oregon_ndvi_timeseries.png to the data store under folder OregonNDVI/output.
